text: Hi, welcome to This is My Architecture. Today I'm with Damien from NextThink. Hi Damien.
Hi Ross.
So what can you tell us about NextThink?
So NextThink is a global leader in digital employee experience. We provide IT teams with
a certain number of capabilities like real-time analytics, automation, and employee feedback
across endpoints. Alright, and what part of NextThink are we going to be looking at today?
So today we are going to look at NextThink experience. So that's our cloud project.
And here, as you can see, we have multiple PCs and multiple regions. So here I have region
A with some components, region B and region C. And we have a specificity. Our solution
is based on a multi-turn platform. And for each customer, we associate a region. This
means that, for example, some customer, a customer will be deployed in region A, could be deployed
in region B and in region C. So of course, the challenge for us is to route the request, the
HTTP request, as soon as possible to the right EKS cluster where the HTTP endpoint is deployed.
So to minimize the impact in terms of latency, we use route 53 and the latency-based routing
to retrieve the DNS to have the IP addresses of our API Gateway. So let's say, for example, this user
will request the IP address, will call directly the API Gateway, and then we need to figure out where do we
need to deploy or to route this request. So the API Gateway will access a Lambda function,
will go directly to DynamoDB to make the association between the tenant UID and the region it belongs to.
OK. I see with DynamoDB here, it doesn't seem related to any particular region. Why is that?
Well, that's a good question. Actually, we are using global table in DynamoDB. So this
concretely means that it's available for all the regions.
OK. And what comes next?
Yeah. Then, as soon as we have the association between the tenant UID and the region, we can route
the request to the right EKS cluster. So in this case, the Lambda could be either in the same region, or we need to go across region. And in this case, we are
using, or we are using the Transit Gateway.
OK.
Then the Transit Gateway, if for example, we say this customer is deployed in the EKS cluster in region C, we will use the
interpeering communication between the two Transit Gateway, and the call will be routed in the EKS cluster here.
OK. So theoretically, that customer could be in region B. So from this, we can say the customer is only associated with a single region.
Yeah.
That obviously makes sense. Why would you have, can you give me an example of a customer that would have their lowest latency would be to region A, but they happen to be in region C?
Yeah, that's a good question, actually. We have a lot of customers which are deployed across the world, and some of them have offices in Asia, in Europe, or in the US.
And during the design phase, we say, OK, it will, I mean, everything will be deployed in region C, in terms of data storage and EKS endpoints.
But of course, if I'm not in the same region, because I belong to another offices, then in this case, I need to go through this process to be routed in the right region.
So that's more or less related to the size of our customer.
All right. Fantastic. Damien, I want to thank you for coming today and sharing this architecture with us. It's been very interesting.
Thanks a lot, Ross.
And thank you for watching This Is My Architecture.
Oooged
Voracate
Ooo
